corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: To address this challenge, we introduce the Beyond the Imitation Game benchmark (BIG- bench).

context_window.next: BIG-bench currently consists of 204 tasks, contributed by 450 authors across 132 institutions.

location.sentence: For instance, benchmarks often propose tasks that codify narrow subsets of areas, such as language understanding (Wang et al., 2019a), summarization (See et al., 2017; Hermann et al., 2015; Narayan et al., 2018; Koupaee & Wang, 2018; Rush et al., 2015; Graff et al., 2003), or trivia-question answering (Joshi et al., 2017; Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Rajpurkar et al., 2016).

context_window.previous: First, many benchmarks have restricted scope, largely targeting a single or a few capabilities on which language models have already proven some proficiency.
context_window.current: For instance, benchmarks often propose tasks that codify narrow subsets of areas, such as language understanding (Wang et al., 2019a), summarization (See et al., 2017; Hermann et al., 2015; Narayan et al., 2018; Koupaee & Wang, 2018; Rush et al., 2015; Graff et al., 2003), or trivia-question answering (Joshi et al., 2017; Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Rajpurkar et al., 2016).
context_window.next: Because they are narrowly targeted, and because their targets are often ones that language models are already known to perform, they are ill-suited to identify new and unexpected capabilities that language models may develop with increased scale, or to characterize the breadth of current capabilities.

location.sentence: All figures in this paper showing performance on individual metrics use one of the following: (as is standard practice, we usually report scores in the range 0-100 unless stated otherwise) • exact_string_match: Accuracy on exact. • multiple_choice_grade: A weighted multiple choice accuracy between 0-100, where a set of targets and scores for each potential target are specified.

context_window.previous: The full list of available metrics for JSON tasks can be found on the BIG-bench repo.
context_window.current: All figures in this paper showing performance on individual metrics use one of the following: (as is standard practice, we usually report scores in the range 0-100 unless stated otherwise) • exact_string_match: Accuracy on exact. • multiple_choice_grade: A weighted multiple choice accuracy between 0-100, where a set of targets and scores for each potential target are specified.
context_window.next: This reduces to standard multiple choice accuracy when a single target is assigned a score of 1 and the rest score 0. • expected_calibration_error: A measure of a model’s calibration – i.e. how well the model’s accuracy matches the probability it assigns to a response. expected_calibration_error is the absolute deviation between the assigned probability and average accuracy, after binning examples in terms of assigned probability (Naeini et al., 2015). • multiple_choice_brier_score: A measure of calibration given as the squared error between model assigned probabilities and 0, 1 targets across classes (Brier, 1950).

location.sentence: Furthermore, models are measured across scales to facilitate a naive extrapolation of the scale at which they may be indistinguishable from human evaluators.

context_window.previous: We provide a human-evaluator baseline and expert human evaluations on this diverse set of tasks, in order to measure whether model performance is broadly distinguishable from human-evaluator performance.
context_window.current: Furthermore, models are measured across scales to facilitate a naive extrapolation of the scale at which they may be indistinguishable from human evaluators.
context_window.next: In homage to Alan Turing’s imitation game (Turing, 1950), and because we aim to extract information about model behavior beyond what is available from a binary judgement of whether a model is distinguishable from a human, we call this benchmark the Beyond the Imitation Game benchmark, or BIG-bench.

top_candidates.sentence: When human-equivalent performance is reached for these benchmarks, they are often either discontinued and replaced or extended through the inclusion of more challenging benchmarks, in a kind of "challenge-solve-and-replace" evaluation dynamic , or a "dataset-solve-and-patch" adversarial benchmark co-evolution .
Different choices in task design can make the same capability appear to stagnate, gradually improve, or suddenly break through.
However, they are able to implicitly judge cause versus effect by assigning higher probability to sentences that present events in the correct order.
These tasks present a context where the completion is ambiguous given the available evidence, or where the model is asked to make generalizations about broad classes.

To achieve a more accurate baseline of human performance on BIG-bench tasks where possible, we relied on a team of expert evaluators who continuously performed evaluation of tasks within the benchmark over the past year.

In order to inform future research, prepare for disruptive new model capabilities, and ameliorate socially harmful effects, it is vital that we understand the present and near-future capabilities and limitations of language models.
To address this challenge, we introduce the Beyond the Imitation Game benchmark (BIGbench).
To address this challenge, we introduce the Beyond the Imitation Game benchmark (BIGbench).
To address this challenge, we introduce the Beyond the Imitation Game benchmark (BIGbench).
A recent consensus is that as generative language models are made larger, and are trained on more data, they perform better in predictable ways.
We also expect continued performance gains from improvements in architecture and training methods.
To address the lack of geographic diversity in NLP, Masakhane used participatory research to construct a machine-translation benchmark for over 30 low-resource languages ).

EleutherAI's Language Model Evaluation Harness is another open-source collaborative benchmarking codebase, though unlike BIG-bench it does not introduce new tasks; it instead organizes existing tasks and defines a unified API for evaluating models on them.
The GEM benchmark is a living benchmark for natural language generation, evaluation, and metrics , constructed by a broad collaboration of more than 50 contributors.
GEM provides an environment in which models can be tested on a wide set of tasks and with different evaluation strategies that can evolve alongside the models.

DynaBench is an open-source platform for dynamic dataset creation and model benchmarking that uses human-and-model-in-the-loop to identify and collect examples where models will fail but humans would not.
SyntaxGym ) is an online platform designed to make targeted evaluations following the norms of psycholinguistic experimental design.
The Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) benchmark is a collection of 57 diverse tasks, collated by 7 authors.
BIG-bench has also been partially evaluated on other models including Gopher , Chinchilla , and T0 .

While Brier score is a proper scoring rule for measuring the accuracy of predicted probabilities, ECE has been widely used to measure calibration due to its intuitive nature. for discussion of properties and drawbacks of various calibration metrics.

Understanding social bias in machine learning systems in general and language models in particular is an important, multifaceted challenge, and it has been the subject of much prior work ; Models perform at chance when asked explicitly to identify which events are cause and effect.
Table App.1 shows the architectures and parameters of the BIG-G models tested on BIG-bench.
